Source_Species.reason: AtRTP5 is explicitly identified as an Arabidopsis thaliana gene throughout the paper.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper explicitly names the source species as Arabidopsis thaliana.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: The wild-type background/ecotype used is explicitly stated as Columbia-0 (Col-0).

Applied_Species.reason: Functional assays were performed in Arabidopsis thaliana and Nicotiana benthamiana.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Both Arabidopsis thaliana and Nicotiana benthamiana are explicitly named in the Results and Methods.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Arabidopsis assays used Col-0; Nicotiana benthamiana variety/cultivar is not specified, so 'NA' is consistent with the paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The Summary and Results state that AtRTP5 encodes a WD40 repeat domain-containing protein.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: GFP-AtRTP5 localization is reported in the nucleus and plasma membrane, supported by DAPI and Myr-mCherry colocalization.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: The paper states AtRTP5 was induced by P. parasitica, up-regulated during the biotrophic phase, and peaked at 24 hpi.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: AtRTP5 induction upon P. parasitica infection was shown in Arabidopsis leaves and confirmed in roots by RNA-seq (Fig. S4); NbRTP5 homologs were also reported as induced in infected N. benthamiana leaves (Fig. S3c).

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Endogenous jasmonic acid (JA) and salicylic acid (SA) contents were directly measured and discussed as regulated by AtRTP5.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: JA and SA are explicitly treated in the paper as plant hormones/hormone signaling components.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Hormone contents were directly measured in 2-week-old seedlings without infection; leaves were used for many expression assays, but the paper does not directly state metabolite accumulation was measured in leaves.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper directly links JA and SA signaling to plant immunity/resistance against Phytophthora pathogens.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show AtRTP5 negatively regulates resistance to Phytophthora, with loss of function reducing JA biosynthesis/signaling and activating SA biosynthesis/signaling, while overexpression shows the opposite hormone trends.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each listed point is supported in Results: mutants are more resistant; overexpression increases susceptibility in Arabidopsis and promotes infection in N. benthamiana; JA decreases and SA increases in rtp5-3 while opposite trends occur in overexpression; nuclear localization is required for function.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are explicitly used: T-DNA mutants, overexpression, VIGS, transient expression, GFP/DAPI localization, infection assays, qRT-PCR, RNA-seq, and HPLC-MS/MS.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These treatments/conditions are explicitly described: P. parasitica infection, P. infestans infection, and uninfected tissues for transcriptome and hormone measurements.

Applied_Promoters.reason: The paper states GFP-AtRTP5 was driven by the CaMV 35S promoter and overexpression constructs included the CaMV 35S promoter.

Omics_Data.reason: Whole-genome resequencing was used to identify the resistant mutant insertion, and RNA-seq is reported with platform Illumina HiSeq4000 PE150 and accession PRJNA562027.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Overexpression increasing susceptibility is directly supported, but framing this as a 'tradeoff' is interpretive; NbRTP5 silencing plants were reported to grow more strongly, yet no explicit tradeoff analysis was performed.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper supports AtRTP5 as a susceptibility factor whose loss enhances resistance, but explicit breeding/editing application value is not directly stated in the Results.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The Results explicitly show nuclear localization is required, and plasma-membrane relocalization abolished the infection-promoting effect.
